mission.title: Install and Configure Elastic Cloud on Kubernetes (ECK)
mission.description: Elastic Cloud on Kubernetes (ECK) automates the deployment and management of the Elastic Stack on Kubernetes. It simplifies the orchestration of Elasticsearch, Kibana, and other Elastic products, making it ideal for users looking to leverage Elastic's capabilities in a Kubernetes environment. Key trade-offs include the need for Kubernetes expertise and potential complexity in managing custom configurations.

steps.title: Add the ECK Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the Elastic Cloud on Kubernetes Helm chart repository to your Helm client.

```bash

helm repo add elastic https://helm.elastic.co

helm repo update

```

> This is the official ECK Helm repository.
> Updates the local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install the ECK operator
steps.description:
This step installs the ECK operator in the 'elastic-system' namespace using Helm.

```bash

kubectl create namespace elastic-system

helm install eck-operator elastic/eck-operator --namespace elastic-system --version 2.14.0

```

> Creates a namespace for ECK.
> Installs the ECK operator with a specific version.

steps.title: Verify the ECK operator installation
steps.description:
This step checks if the ECK operator is running successfully.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n elastic-system

```

> Lists all pods in the elastic-system namespace to verify the operator is running.

steps.title: Create an Elasticsearch cluster
steps.description:
This step deploys an Elasticsearch cluster using a custom resource definition (CRD).

```bash

cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: elasticsearch.k8s.elastic.co/v1
kind: Elasticsearch
metadata:
  name: my-elasticsearch
  namespace: default
spec:
  version: 8.0.0
  nodeSets:
  - name: default
    count: 3
    config:
      node.store.type: mmapfs
EOF

```

> Creates an Elasticsearch cluster named 'my-elasticsearch'.
commands:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: elasticsearch.k8s.elastic.co/v1
kind: Elasticsearch
metadata:
  name: my-elasticsearch
  namespace: default
spec:
  version: 8.0.0
  nodeSets:
  - name: default
    count: 3
    config:
      node.store.type: mmapfs
EOF

steps.title: Verify the Elasticsearch cluster
steps.description:
This step checks the status of the Elasticsearch cluster to ensure it is running.

```bash

kubectl get elasticsearch -n default

```

> Lists Elasticsearch resources to verify the cluster status.

uninstall.title: Uninstall the ECK operator
uninstall.description:
This step removes the ECK operator and all associated resources. Be aware that this may lead to data loss if the Elasticsearch cluster is not backed up.

```bash

helm uninstall eck-operator --namespace elastic-system

kubectl delete namespace elastic-system

```

> Uninstalls the ECK operator.
> Deletes the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade the ECK operator
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you have a backup of your Elasticsearch data. This step upgrades the ECK operator to a newer version.

```bash

kubectl drain <node-name> --ignore-daemonsets

helm upgrade eck-operator elastic/eck-operator --namespace elastic-system --version 2.14.1

kubectl uncordon <node-name>

```

> Drains the node to prepare for upgrade.
> Upgrades the ECK operator to the specified version.
> Uncordons the node after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to create Elasticsearch cluster'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur due to insufficient resources or misconfiguration.

**Fix:**
Check the resource requests and limits in your Elasticsearch CRD and ensure your cluster has enough resources.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'ECK operator not running'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The ECK operator pod may have failed to start due to configuration issues.

**Fix:**
Check the logs of the ECK operator pod using 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n elastic-system'.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Elasticsearch cluster is not healthy'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This can happen if the nodes are not able to communicate or if there are resource constraints.

**Fix:**
Verify the network policies and resource allocation for the Elasticsearch nodes.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to connect to Kibana'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** Kibana may not be properly configured or the Elasticsearch cluster is down.

**Fix:**
Check the Kibana logs and ensure the Elasticsearch cluster is running and accessible.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for Elasticsearch 9.x and improved TLS certificate management.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from 2.13, ensure to update your Elasticsearch CRDs to the new version.

resolution.summary: You should see the Elasticsearch cluster status as 'green' when everything is working: 'kubectl get elasticsearch -n default'.

kind: Elasticsearch
metadata:

  namespace: default
spec:

  - name: default

EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster with sufficient resources and the required tools installed.
